data.title: 3 Steps To Retiring With $1 Million In High-Yield Stocks
data.description:
Co-produced by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

Retirement naturally comes with mixed emotions. It is the end of an era, but also the beginning of a new one.

In the midst of such a big life change, it is helpful to have as much certainty and peace of mind about one's nest egg as possible.

The standard advice is to follow the “4% rule”—withdraw 4% of your portfolio value per year, every year. But there are downsides to this strategy. While it works well during bull markets when stocks are rising much more than 4% per year, it can be destructive to one's portfolio value during recessions and bull markets when retirees need to sell beaten-down stocks to fund living expenses.

Another approach is to invest one's nest egg into higher-yielding yet fundamentally solid stocks and live entirely from the dividend income, leaving the principal untouched.

If you have $1 million saved and are attracted to this approach, or if you are somewhere along in the process of accumulating $1 million and want to eventually retire this way, here are three simple steps to follow.

Step 1: Plan

First and foremost, you need to determine how much annual income you want or need.

This depends on many factors.

  • Have you completely paid off your mortgage?
  • Are you in good health? It's always a good idea to assume that no matter your age, your healthcare spending is probably only going up from here.
  • Do you expect any major spending items (such as a new car or family member's college tuition) in the coming years?
  • Do you have any other sources of income, such as Social Security, a pension, rental properties, part-time work, etc.?

One retiree's passive income needs may be very different than another's. One may need only $40,000 for a comfortable retirement, while another may need $80,000.

Step 2: Build

The goal of the entire portfolio of one's investable assets is maximal yet stable income generation.

To minimize the risk of needing to sell stock to pay for living expenses, we recommend maintaining a healthy cash cushion. Financial advisors typically suggest retirees keep at least one year's worth of living expenses in cash. The purpose of this cash cushion is to (1) cover any big, unexpected expenses, and to (2) fill the gap with lower income from dividend cuts during recessions.

Fortunately for income investors in today's environment, though, holding cash equivalents does not entirely take that capital out of the game. Many cash equivalent funds offer relatively generous yields. Here are some examples along with their current 30-day SEC yields:

  • iShares 0-3 Month Treasury ETF (SGOV) - 4.2%
  • iShares Ultra Short-Term Bond Active ETF (ICSH) - 4.6%
  • JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST) - 4.5%
  • Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (VUSB) - 4.7%
  • Vanguard Ultra-Short Treasury ETF (VGUS) - 4.2%

Of course, the Fed is expected to cut their key policy rate at least 2–3 times this year, which will cause these cash equivalent ETFs' yields to decline. But investors can probably expect 3% or higher yields from them for the foreseeable future.

Once the cash cushion is in place, it's time to build out the rest of the investment portfolio.

As we explained in “Why Retirees Don't Need A Bond Allocation,” we think retirees would do well to forgo bonds and bond funds (aside from the cash cushion) and focus on stocks. While bonds offer income stability during recessions, they also provide zero income growth, leaving coupon income vulnerable to the slow erosion of inflation.

Retirees need to maintain their real purchasing power not just for a year or two but for multiple decades. Bonds can only help with that if they generate excess income (beyond what's necessary to fund living expenses) that can be reinvested into more income-generating assets.

We would start by establishing a solid and diversified base of ETFs that provide either reliable dividend growth or high yields.

Here are some options we would gravitate toward:

ETF Dividend Yield Estimated Dividend Growth Rate
Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) 3.6% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) 3.5% Mid-Single-Digit
Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit
JP Morgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) 7.5% Flat
Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) 9.4% Flat
VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) 10.8% Flat

This is basically a barbell approach, with the first three ETFs holding a diversified set of dividend growth stocks and the latter three ETFs employing various high-yielding strategies.

SCHD is often considered the “gold standard” dividend ETF for its history of generating double-digit dividend growth combined with an above-average dividend yield.

HDV follows a very similar moderately high-yielding strategy but with more emphasis on energy companies and defensive sectors like consumer staples and utilities.

MLPX owns a small basket of high-quality midstream oil & gas companies that own pipelines, storage facilities, and processing plants.

JEPI employs a unique options-writing strategy on top of an actively managed portfolio of dividend-paying stocks to generate options premiums on top of the portfolio's dividends.

PFFA is an actively managed fund that invests in preferred equities, which are a hybrid between common stocks and bonds. The ETF's active managers have generated the best total returns of any preferred ETF.

Finally, BIZD owns a basket of business development companies ("BDCs"), which are non-bank lenders that make loans to private, middle-market businesses. We find BDCs to be a good way for the average individual investor to gain exposure to the growing private credit industry.

Beyond the base of ETFs to provide stability and diversification, we would then enhance the portfolio with a group of individual dividend stocks.

The key with individual stocks is to balance yield with dividend safety. Many income investors make the mistake of buying ultra-high-yielding stocks that ultimately prove to be value traps with unsustainable dividends.

A good place to start looking is among dividend aristocrats, contenders, and challengers.

  • Dividend Aristocrats: 25+ consecutive years of dividend growth
  • Dividend Contenders: 10–24 years of consecutive dividend growth
  • Dividend Challengers: 5–9 years of consecutive dividend growth

There are multiple lists on the Internet of such stocks, one of which can be found here.

We would also own at least a handful of real estate investment trusts ("REITs") (VNQ), focusing again on dividend growth track record, payout ratio, balance sheet strength, and portfolio quality.

Some blue-chips in the REIT space we would gravitate toward include:

  • Agree Realty (ADC) - best-in-class single-tenant net lease retail properties
  • Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) - top-tier life science campuses
  • Sila Realty Trust (SILA) - single-tenant medical buildings
  • VICI Properties (VICI) - Las Vegas & regional casino resorts
  • W.P. Carey (WPC) - single-tenant industrial and logistics properties

The blend of ETFs, dividend growth stocks, and REITs will depend a lot on the individual investor's risk tolerance, income needs, and sphere of competence.

Step 3: Maintain

There is both an art and a science to maintaining an investment portfolio once it is built.

It starts with the frequency with which one checks their portfolio performance. Too frequently checking one's account often leads to over-trading, triggering lots of taxable transactions without adding much value. But too infrequently checking one's portfolio can lead to greater losses in cases where a company's financials slowly erode and increase the likelihood of a dividend cut.

Checking one's portfolio around once a week strikes us as a pretty good balance.

Of course, portfolio maintenance means more than just checking ticker prices. In fact, ticker prices can be misleading by diverging from the fundamentals for a while. What should matter most to investors are the fundamentals: revenue, earnings, free cash flow, dividend coverage, how management are allocating capital, and so on.

It pays for a DIY retiree to devote the time to become proficient in business fundamentals, at least for their own holdings.

Fortunately, there are many tools available to help investors with that nowadays, including websites like Seeking Alpha, AI-generated summaries of earnings reports and conference calls, and investor presentations on public companies' investor relations websites.

By maintaining a balanced portfolio, selectively adding to solid holdings in the midst of pullbacks, and only selling stocks when they are at elevated risk of dividend cuts, retirees can efficiently manage their nest eggs without letting it take over their lives.

Bottom Line

Retiring on your own, self-managed nest egg of $1 million is by no means easy or carefree, but it is doable. Most people end up giving their money to financial advisors, which is a perfectly reasonable approach. But one can save a small fortune in advisory fees by managing their nest egg themselves.

By following the three simple steps above, investors can put themselves in a very strong position to comfortably retire with both peace of mind and the knowledge that their principal is remaining intact.

Of course, we here at High Yield Investor make it our life's work to help individual investors achieve their dream retirement. Our Retirement Portfolio is intended to do just that, and we are very pleased with its results so far.

data.title: Diageo: Better Than Expected Tariff Hit Meets A 50% Drawdown
data.description:
Diageo (NYSE:DEO) shares were in a 45% drawdown before Trump was elected, primarily due to lower alcohol consumption worldwide, declining sales, and shrinking margins. Trump's tariffs exacerbated the selloff, and shares are now trading 50% below all-time highs.

However, Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs were not as bad as expected for the company.

With the stock trading around its 10-year lows and back at 2012 levels, it's time to revisit our bearish stance.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To Diageo

Diageo is one of the largest alcoholic beverage sellers in the world, with over $20 billion in annual sales. The British company sells every type of spirit you can think of, with global brands like Don Julio, Captain Morgan, Guinness, Johnnie Walker, Smirnoff, and others.

I've been covering the company on Seeking Alpha since 2023, mostly with a bearish view, given the company's lack of growth prospects and relatively high valuation.

Getting To Know The Industry

The alcohol industry has two major end-point customers. One is the at-home drinker, and one is the out-of-home drinker. The majority of sales include a middle-men, and Diageo has effectively no direct-to-consumer relationships.

The strength of the industry is highly reliant on good inventory management from all players. During Covid, Diageo and its peers got a major boost, as people were buying alcohol for their homes. Think about it, instead of one gin bottle serving the entire customer base of a local bar for a few days, all these customers bought one bottle each, resulting in faster depletions and replenishment cycles.

Also, alcohol consumption was high because people were working from home, and didn't worry as much about being hungover at work in the following day.

These trends pushed Diageo shares to an all-time high at $220 right at the end of 2021 and the last of the Covid lockdowns.

Diageo Had Enough Problems Even Without Tariffs

After achieving the 2021 highs, restaurants and bar were back open, and people started going back outside, whether it was work, travel, or just going out. The industry was caught off-guard with huge inventories, as it planned for continued strength, like it saw in the Covid years.

Meanwhile, many celebrities and influencers created their own alcohol brands during Covid, taking market share from the large players. In addition, it turns out, people aren't drinking as much as they used to, especially Gen Z.

Diageo sales have been essentially flat since 2022. In the half that ended in December (the company's fiscal H1-25), organic growth was 1%, driven by price and mix, though net sales dropped by 1%.

Meanwhile, overhead and strategic investments are dragging down margins, with operating profit down 4.9% Y/Y, or down 1.2% organically. EPS dropped even further, by about 10%, and net leverage increased to 3.1x adjusted EBITDA.

As you can see, Diageo and the entire industry are pressured, which has led the company to cut its medium-term guidance.

The problems have started even before any tariff-related headwinds, and then, they arrived.

Better Than Expected Tariff Hit

From Diageo's earnings release:

On Saturday 1 February 2025, President Trump in one of three separate Executive Orders announced the implementation of 25% tariffs on goods imported into the US from Canada and Mexico. These tariffs, applicable from 4 February 2025, would apply to our products made in Canada and Mexico in transit after midnight 1 February 2025.

In the US, our largest market, the products which would be impacted by the tariffs would mainly be our tequila portfolio, which given geographic origin requirements must be made in Mexico, and also Canadian whisky.

Before Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs, the market was worried about additional tariffs on Mexico and Canada, as well as higher tariffs on other countries where Diageo produces.

Now, it appears about 55% of Diageo's sales will be completely exempt from tariffs, and that only about 2% of the company's profits are expected to be hit.

When it comes to measuring the tariff threat priced into the stock, my estimate is that it was about a 9% hit:

On one hand, Diageo performed better than the S&P 500, so you can argue no specific hit. However, consumer staples have been outperforming the market by 15%, and Brown-Forman (BF.B), an alcoholic beverage seller that does most of its production in the US, has been flat.

This makes me think that if it weren't for tariffs, Diageo would probably be flat as well.

Valuation

So, the tariff threat is not as bad as the market thought. However, does that, by itself, make Diageo an attractive investment? I'm sceptical.

At $108 a share, Diageo is trading at 17 times forward earnings, and 16 times '26 estimates, with expectations for low-single digit revenue growth at best in the foreseeable future.

This places Diageo right in line with other beverage peers, all of which are expected to generate better growth. And, the structural headwinds of the alcohol industry remain.

To me, this reflects that Diageo is hovering around fair valuation.

Conclusion

Diageo has been losing market share in the pressured alcohol industry since 2021. The company's sales have been flat, and margins have declined.

These problems are structural, and made me a bear even before tariffs.

Now, although the tariff threat turned out to be better than expected (for now), I don't see a reason to be excited about the stock. Growth limitations persist, and valuation isn't too attractive either.

In my view, Diageo can be a near-term low-risk play for a volatile market, but I'd much rather look for opportunities elsewhere amid the recent sharp market selloff.

Therefore, I reiterate Diageo as a 'Hold'.

data.title: Dell: An Undervalued AI Play
data.description:
Dell Technologies (DELL) is executing well in the server market and reporting strong growth in its Infrastructure Solutions Group as a result. With spending on AI products only set to ramp up, Dell is well-positioned to capitalize on growing spending, especially on servers and networking products, and I see a strong upside revaluation case for Dell in the years ahead. Dell’s Infrastructure Solutions Group has also emerged as a major catalyst for an operating income uplift, which could push shares of the hardware company into a new up-leg. Since shares of Dell are only trading at 9.0X forward earnings, I believe the hardware company is undervalued and could be an attractive play on both the AI and server markets.

Previous rating

I called Dell a "Deep Value AI Stock" in September 2024 due to the company’s exposure to the Data Center market and AI, and its relatively low valuation based off of earnings. What remains highly attractive about Dell is that the company has a growing server business which I expect to benefit handsomely from higher, and potentially accelerating, investments in the Data Center market. With Dell trading at a relatively low 9X P/E ratio, I believe the hardware company is looking at a multi-year run-way of high EPS and cash flow growth.

Growth and revaluation potential driven by growing server demand in Dell's ISG

Dell managed to turn its business around and achieve positive top-line growth in FY 2025 due to massive investments in AI-optimized server products. Dell reported 95.6B in total revenue for FY 2025, showing 8% growth and a 22 PP swing in the top-line growth rate last year, mainly due to escalating demand for AI-optimized servers and other networking products. In fact, Dell's server and networking business, which is grouped together in the Infrastructure Solutions Group, is seeing exceptional strong growth and the segment has emerged as a catalyst for an operating income surge.

Therefore, the Infrastructure Solutions Group is the core element of my bullish outlook for the hardware company as well.

As I indicated in my last work on the hardware company, advancements in AI are pushing the entire server market into a new upgrade cycle, which is where Dell has a lever to grow its earnings.

In the fourth-quarter, Dell’s Infrastructure Solutions Group grew its top line 22% Y/Y to $11.4B, but its servers and networking products experienced a significantly more powerful revenue boost: they grew 37% year-over-year while the segment’s operating income surged twice as fast as its segment top line (+44%).

Infrastructure Solutions Group is currently lifting up Dell's entire business and with AI investments expected to grow fast in the future, ISG will remain instrumental in helping Dell grow both its top line and operating income. In fact, ISG has become so significant for Dell's business represented 46% of Dell's FY 2025 revenue and 65% of total non-GAAP operating income.

Dell’s long term operational model calls for 3-4% growth in its top line, with the out-performing Infrastructure Solutions Group expected to grow twice as fast at 6-8%. Therefore, AI and server spending will remain key to Dell being able to grow both its earnings, as well as, its free cash flow.

Dell’s valuation

Shares of Dell are quite cheap considering that the hardware company has significant exposure to the fast-growing server market which is experiencing strong tailwinds from advancements in artificial intelligence. Dell’s shares are currently valued at a price-to-earnings ratio -- based off of FY 2026 earnings -- of 9.0X compared to 7.7X for Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), 7.5X for HP (HP) and 9.6X for Super Micro Computer (SMCI). The industry group average P/E ratio is 8.5X.

Super Micro Computer recently resolved its filing issues, which created a significant negative sentiment overhang in FY 2024. With Super Micro Computer finally submitting its delayed financial statements, shares have upside potential as well. In my opinion, Super Micro Computer is likely the most attractive server play for investors given its 95% revenue exposure to the server market as well as the positive change in investor sentiment lately: Out Of The Woods (Rating Upgrade).

As a leading hardware company with a sizable server and networking business, Dell could revalue, as I indicated in my last work, to a ~15X P/E ratio (and I made the same argument with regard to Super Micro Computer), considering that both companies are highly exposed to server segment tailwinds. In the case a 15X P/E ratio could be realized, Dell may have a fair value in the neighborhood of $158 per-share (assuming a consensus FY 2026 EPS estimate of $10.58 per-share). A revaluation may happen if Dell sees an acceleration of top-line growth in ISG as well as stronger operating income growth related to higher server investments.

Risks with Dell

The biggest risk for Dell, as I see it, relates to a potential slowdown in the server market as well as a falling share of operating income earned by the Infrastructure Solutions Group. Dell is growing its business, but currently depends on ISG to propel the entire business forward. If companies were to scale back spending on AI/servers/server racks, Dell's business may more depend on the slow-growing Client Solutions Group which sells notebooks and PCs, which would pose a risk to Dell's earnings and free cash flow growth prospects.

Final thoughts

Dell is a fast-growing, server-focused hardware company that is seeing massive momentum in its server and networking business. In fact, operating income in the Infrastructure Solutions Group, which represents 50% of consolidated revenue, is growing its segment operating profits twice the rate of its top-line growth. Dell’s ISG was also responsible for a massive 65% share of FY 2025 operating income and its operating income growth will chiefly depend on the server and networking business going forward. Dell’s operational framework calls for ISG to continue to out-perform CSG (in terms of top-line growth). I like Dell’s low valuation based off of earnings and see a case for accelerating earnings growth as long as companies keep spending money on new AI and server products.

data.title: Can't Put Toothpaste Back In The Tube: Tariff, Recession And Bear Market
data.description:
Despite the one-day decline of 4.84% on April 3, market prices indicate that most market participants believe that President Trump’s tariffs are merely a bluff and that they will soon be negotiated down to almost nothing after the US’s trading partners offer concessions.

Reasonable people can differ about what the ultimate intentions of Trump and his economic advisors have been – and I will not be addressing this issue. The issue I will address is the fact that “the toothpaste cannot be put back into the tube.” Intentional or not, a US business cycle recession is now likely. And negotiations are unlikely to alter this outcome – although they could modify the intensity.

The US Economy is Already Contracting

According to the GDP Nowcast of the Atlanta Fed, the US economy is likely to register a contraction of around -1.0% in the first quarter of 2025 (excluding the anomalous spike in gold imports).

Figure 1: GDP Nowcast for 1Q 2025

This nowcast mainly reflects data pertaining to the months of January and February and a small amount of “soft data” from March. Therefore, the impact of Trump’s tariffs is not reflected in this data. The impact of the tariffs will mostly start to be reflected in data that will be published in May.

The key point to understand is that at the moment that tariffs begin to impact the US economy, the US economy will have already stalled to around 0% growth, or it might have even been contracting. This means that tariffs will represent an incremental hit to an already weakened US economy.

What Will the Impact of Tariffs Be?

Economists currently estimate that, if fully implemented, US tariffs and countermeasures by other nations will have a negative impact on US GDP of roughly 0.5% to 1.5%.

In my view, this greatly underestimates the impact of the tariff shock because it does not account for disruptions that will be caused in supply chains nor for the secondary negative impacts on investment – both fixed investment and inventory investment. My estimate is that if tariffs and countermeasures were fully implemented, the impact would be more on the order of -2.0% to -3.5% when all knock-on effects are taken into account.

Now the big question is: To what extent will the tariffs actually be implemented and for how long? Many believe that negotiations will quickly reduce the tariffs to almost nothing. Some even believe that the tariffs will cause trade barriers to fall below levels that they were at prior to the tariffs being implemented.

Can The Toothpaste be Put Back into the Tube?

In our view, many nations will manage to negotiate lower tariffs and some bilateral trade barriers may actually be negotiated to levels below where they were before “liberation day.” However, it is our view that it is highly unrealistic to think that tariff barriers and other counter-measures will not disrupt the global economy in the next 6 to 12 months.

First of all, it is our view that Trump's new “base tariff rate” of 10% is unlikely to be eliminated for most nations. We think that this base rate is going to remain as a central fixture of Trump’s policy. Second, while we do think that “reciprocal tariffs” are more open to be negotiated downwards, we think that this will be much more difficult to accomplish that most currently assume. Since the reciprocal tariffs are mainly targeting non-tariff barriers and currency manipulation in nations such as China, Korea and Japan, we do not think that there is much prospect that disputes over these highly contentious issues can be resolved within a short timeframe, if at all.

Therefore, the toothpaste has been squeezed out of the tube and is now splattered on the counter. And while some of it can be put back with difficulty, much of is unlikely to ever be put back during this administration.

We do not believe that there is a viable path to get back to the status quo in trade relations with Europe, China, Japan and many other Asian nations. Indeed, the idea of returning to the status quo is almost absurd. Trump is essentially gambling his legacy on the success of this policy. There will be no return to the status quo for him. Nations will either have to cave into his demands (reasonable or not) and give him a political victory, or bear the consequences. Trump and his team have decided that they are willing to bear the consequences of a 1.0%-2.0% hit to GDP because they calculate that nations that enjoy a trade surplus with the US stand much more to lose.

The Impact on the US Stock Market

Our base case is that roughly half of the tariffs will be negotiated away in exchange for concessions and that the impact of the tariff barriers and countermeasures that remain will be on the order of -1.0% to -2.0%. This is enough to push the already decelerating US economy into a mild recession that could last 6 to 12 months. In such a scenario, as can be seen in Figure 2, we think that the S&P 500 could decline by roughly 52.4% to a level of about 2925.

Figure 2: Bear Market Scenarios

Some may be somewhat incredulous that a relatively mild recession could cause a stock market decline of over 50%. But this is exactly what happened between in the bear market that started in 2001. The recession of 2001 was one of the mildest in US economic history measured by indicators such as GDP and employment. Yet the S&P 500 declined by more than 50%.

This has been a fairly common pattern in US economic history. The depth and duration of a bear market decline is generally related to two main factors: The intensity of the associated economic disruption and the starting level of valuation. When the starting level of valuation of the equity market is high, the peak-to-trough decline in the market is usually very severe, even if the associated decline in economic activity is relatively mild.

For this reason, we think that even a mild business cycle recession is likely to cause an S&P 500 decline of more than -40%. For example, a mild recession, and an associated rapid recovery of EPS -- combined with a partial normalization of the forward PE ratio to 18 – would imply a trough price of 3510 on the S&P 500, or a peak-to-trough decline of 42.9%

For reference, the median forward PE of the S&P 500 (based on GAAP EPS) since 1960 has been roughly 17 while the average was roughly 15.5. It is hardly a stretch to imagine that the S&P 500 PE could fall to these levels in the context of a business cycle recession.

Indeed, the PE ratio on normalized GAAP EPS has fallen much further than that in virtually all previous bear markets associated with a recession.

Overvalued stocks such as Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT) could easily fall more than 50% and still be above their intrinsic fair values and historical trough valuations.

Conclusion

All of the toothpaste that has been spilled cannot be put back into the tube. Given the already weakened condition of the US economy, a partial roll-back of tariffs will not be enough to prevent a recession of some level of intensity.

Our base case is that roughly half of the tariffs will be rolled back in exchange for concessions and that the impact of the tariff barriers and countermeasures that remain will be on the order of -1.0% to -2.0%. This will be enough to push the already decelerating US economy into a mild recession that could last 6 to 12 months.

However, this is not the end of the story. In my next article I will discuss the impacts of other policy and macro shocks that could cause a much more severe recession.

Final Thoughts

At Successful Portfolio Strategy we keenly understand that bear markets represent the single best opportunity that most investors will ever have to boost their long-term returns to above-average levels. An investor will earn more money from side-stepping a bear market than they normally will from picking stocks correctly for 5 straight years. Why? Approximately speaking, when you sidestep a bear market, then buy a stock that is 50% lower, and the stock recovers, you just made a gain of 100%. If you are at or near a market peak (as the market is now), when valuations are already high, it will usually take more than 10 years to make 100% picking stocks. By contrast, if you buy a stock that is 50% down, and it recovers in two years, you will have accomplished in 1 year what it normally takes 5 years to accomplish.

We are prepared to do exactly that in our Investment Group. Not only that, we will be implementing specific strategies designed to directly capitalize on the declining phase of the market and even potential bear market rallies.

Regardless of what your own strategy is, now is not a time to be passive. Ignore the propaganda that tells you to hold on to stocks, no matter how overvalued stocks are and no matter what the circumstances are. This sort of stubborn and prideful attachment to your stocks is illogical and does not serve you. As Warren Buffett says, "You've got all these feelings… The stock doesn't know you own it…. So any feeling I have about the market is not reciprocated."

If you believe, as we do, that stocks have much farther to fall, then now is the time to devise and implement a plan of action.

data.title: Ubisoft: Discipline Pressure From Tencent May Unlock Value
data.description:
Ubisoft (OTCPK:UBSFY; OTCPK:UBSFF) has entered into an agreement [1, 2] to carve out a subsidiary that will be in 25% ownership of Tencent for €1.16B and contain the rights of the largest titles of the company, including the Assassin's Creed, Rainbow Six, and Far Cry series. Investors will now essentially be investing in a holding that has a claim on 75% of the largest contributing assets. Maintaining all else constant, the value of the equity effectively dropped 25%, but investors will be re-pricing the company under the new ownership structure, which could solve some problems and unlock additional value. Note that Tencent already owns 9.7% of Ubisoft, so the 25% in the carved-out entity comes in addition to the current stake.

In a turn of events like this, it is not immediately clear whether this is a good or bad thing for investors. The €1.16B injection can be seen as a lifeline, but if mismanaged, it will only postpone a decline.

For investors, this may not be a bad thing, as Ubisoft will now be pressured to focus on profitable projects, which necessitates the production of games that customers will be enthusiastic about. In a sense, this will impose discipline on the company and pressure them to focus on creating products that sell.

Additionally, there is the question of restructuring. Ubisoft mentioned that they want a leaner organisation, and the creation of the separate entity may allow the company to close a significant portion of the job positions. Such a move may have otherwise been more difficult due to labor laws. If allowed, Ubisoft can close existing positions in the company and reopen them in the subsidiary. On a relative basis, Ubisoft has much less revenue per employee than some of its peers:

  • Ubisoft: €145,3K = €2.137B TTM revenue / 18.7K employees

  • Take Two: $439,5K = $5.45B / 124K (including hiring ramp after 2022)

  • EA: $536,3K = $7,347B /13.7K

  • CD Projekt (2021): $477,5K = zl2,13B ($573M) /1.2K

  • Paradox: 375K = SEK2.2B ($220M) / 587

While these numbers should be taken with caution, as companies can have different classifications on what counts as a full-time equivalent employee (inclusive or exclusive of contractors), it still looks like Ubisoft is lagging some 3x behind competitors in productivity. Should this assumption turn out to be correct, and a large restructuring is coming down the line, then the company may transform into a much more profitable entity.

Most of the gaming industry needs profit margins above 20% to account for the failure risk of games, while Ubisoft has been hovering around single-digit profitability in the last 10 years. By fundamental metrics, Ubisoft is a badly run business, and the new 25% Tencent ownership may force management to impose discipline and an appropriate expense structure.

A Strategic And Financial Play By Tencent

Tencent’s motivation behind the purchase could be as strategic in nature as profit driven. The strategic playbook is likely the same that companies from China use to get a foothold in Western markets, the example with Volvo Car (OTCPK:VLVCY), which opened the auto market to Chinese ownership. Should the case of Ubisoft be similar, I expect that Tencent would be more interested in Ubisoft’s distribution power as they can use it to cross-promote media to Western markets. From a financial aspect, Tencent may be able to unlock value by forcing a rigorous discipline structure that will produce profit margins close to industry averages.

For reference, here are the net profit margins of some of the leading gaming companies in the last 12 months:

  • Ubisoft -2.6%

  • EA 14.3%

  • Take Two -67%

  • CD Projekt 47.7%

  • Paradox 26.6%

  • Capcom 23.5%

  • Tencent 29.4%

Note, Take-Two is ramping development expenses for the new GTA 6, otherwise tends to have margins between 6% and 19%.

Should Tencent succeed in pressuring Ubisoft to transform, then, in my view, a more profitable entity would start moving towards 10% to 15% profit margins [15%-20% operating margins] over 3 years – implying a bottom line between $250M and $375M by the end of 2027.

In the press Q&A, the question arose as to who would lead the new entity and whether the management team would be independent. The response wasn’t so clear, as the CEO said that the entity will have dedicated management but is controlled by Ubisoft. In my view, the entity will indeed have some independence in development and creative direction, which will limit direct influence from Ubisoft, but the projects, direction, and accountability will ultimately be decided by Ubisoft. Finally, the formal structure isn’t as important as the agreement between Tencent and Ubisoft.

Ubisoft Will Need To Rely On Existing Titles To Sustain Revenue

In my view, Ubisoft continues to operate in “reaction” mode, attempting to fix internal problems that have been piling on for years.

Additionally, there is something to be said about treating a title as a cash machine, which seems to be Ubisoft’s recipe for games like Far Cry, Assassin’s Creed, Rainbow Six, Anno, etc. Namely, the company would either develop or acquire a title, and once it peaks, they create iterations that extend the initial enthusiasm. The problem here is that there is a mismatch between the pricing and the content, i.e., the pricing is set at the level of a new game, while the content is incremental with a recycled core. This recipe can work for some time, but the company needs to know when to stop and set its sights on a new origins title. This leads me back to my base thesis on gaming, that replicating success for new titles is the most difficult aspect of the business. Something that requires stepping out of management’s comfort zone and steering the business as a creative endeavor, not just a product portfolio.

In order for Ubisoft to break out of the reactionary pattern, it needs to produce something notable and original, and once it does that, it will be a few iterations before the new title peaks and investors manage to see a return. For this reason, I believe that the upcoming lineup of games will be geared toward sustaining revenues, only producing low- to mid-growth.

The situation is further complicated by Ubisoft’s debt level, with a book value of some €2.3B, indicating a debt to equity of 150%. Most of the debt is covered by cash; however, Ubisoft's cash balance has declined by some €600M from €1.53B in Q3’23 to €933M in the last quarter. The combination of a decreasing cash cushion and a debt-to-equity ratio of 150% may limit Ubisoft’s capacity to take on new ambitious gaming projects and force them to resort to keep flipping existing titles before being able to attempt new growth projects. This is also evident in their relatively short release cycles compared to industry peers.

Getting Past Assassin’s Creed Shadows

The choice to delay the release of Assassin's Creed Shadows allowed the company to produce a decent game and improve the title given some of its legitimate criticisms. This has led to interesting developments, where one camp of customers feels like the game was mediocre but playable, while the extremes on both ends are clambering to find and sometimes contrive arguments on why the game is either extraordinarily good or extraordinarily bad. This new divide is leading to people acknowledging that there is a push for outrage on both sides and giving Ubisoft a second chance.

The response from the company is a good one, and hopefully, one that includes lessons learned. Namely, Ubisoft is trying to quickly move on from the title and will produce two new Assassin's Creed titles in relatively quick succession: Assassin’s Creed Jade (a mobile game set in China), and Assassin's Creed Hexe (1, 2), likely set around the period of the 16th century Germany.

Valuation Model

In the carve-out announcement, Tencent valued the company at an enterprise value of around €4B. With a net debt of €1.4B, the implied market value from the deal comes up to €2.6B or €20 per share. If investors bought into this vision, then they can expect some 72% upside from the current €11.6 per share on the original exchange.

In my valuation, I will assume that Tencent will impose discipline, restructuring, and consequently a sustainable profit margin. Note that I will convert all metrics to USD.

Below, I outline my valuation model components and projections.

Revenue

I project next year’s sales roughly in line with estimates, as I expect that the negative sentiment will subside. Going forward, I expect Ubisoft to roll out titles with the aim of retaining revenue. After a recovery period, I expect the company to be able to release growth games that will sustainably lift the revenue base.

Operating margins

I expect the company to address some of its profitability issues with internal reorganizations. However, the only true profitability increase will come from quality products. I forecast an eventual turnaround, with a net profit stabilization by 2028, and one more downcycle in the remaining years of the forecast period.

Reinvestment

The company’s development costs are included in the operating margins and represent the main capital asset tool for Ubisoft. I do not forecast meaningful growth acquisitions in the future, as that strategy has proven to be value destructive for the company, and expect that their Tencent partners will be against it.

In my model, I have increased the number of outstanding [OTCPK] shares from 670M by 25% in order to reflect the new dilution.

My key assumption is that Tencent will fundamentally steer the company towards discipline and producing games that customers want. This leads to a potential value unlock of around $2.2B. My resulting intrinsic value for Ubisoft post dilution is $3.7B, around a 140% upside from the current $1.52B market capitalization. Converted to price per share, it results in $4.4 for the ticker UBSFY and $24 for the ticker UBSFF.

Unfortunately, Ubisoft’s stock is currently in decline, and the risk to reward asymmetry may not justify preemptively buying the stock. Instead, investors should position themselves in a wait-and-see mode, where they monitor for leading indicators of customer enthusiasm for upcoming titles. I believe that a restructuring is coming down the road, that is not enough to create a good product; the company needs to fundamentally change its production philosophy.

My valuation model does not reflect the current state of Ubisoft, but assumes successful execution of a production turnaround as well as restructuring that brings them to profitability. For this reason, despite finding the company undervalued, I would caution a “hold” rating and monitor for incremental improvements.

Risks

  • My revenue projections assume that future IP iterations are sufficient to sustain revenue levels with modest growth. I also assume that Ubisoft will develop a new successful IP in 7 years.

  • The company has a history of profitability with a central tendency around 0%, indicating bad investments in projects. Despite the new minority partners, there is no guarantee that Ubisoft will be able to turn operations for the better.

  • Selling games in the Chinese market comes with its own set of challenges, including the population’s restrictions on entertainment consumption.

  • After years of operation, Ubisoft produces almost no cash return [dividends or buybacks] to equity investors, and their rising debt levels will prioritize bondholders instead of equity investors.

  • Competitors are taking market share that Ubisoft promised to deliver. For example, while Ubisoft virtually abandoned the Heroes IP, Paradox is re-energizing fans of the genre with their Age of Wonders IP, indicating that it was a production failure on the side of Ubisoft, not lack of demand that resulted in the decline of the series.

  • While previous iterations of Assassin's Creed were effectively monopoly classes within their space, now there are peers that produce equivalent titles and push Ubisoft’s customers into comparative mode when deciding on which games to buy. A recent example of this is the comparison between AC Shadows and Ghost of Tsushima.

  • Ubisoft finds itself in a development environment with lowered barriers to entry and an increasing number of AA and AAA competitors. This means that the company cannot differentiate its products based on 3D aesthetics alone.

  • B2C and B2B gaming engines like Unity and Unreal Engine are further lowering the technological moat of Ubisoft, and introducing even more competitors in the landscape. The company now has to contend with actors that are monetizing Ubisoft’s detractors by releasing meme games.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Of My Parents' All-Time Favorite Retirement Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

Investing is hard. For many reasons.

It's more than getting the money to invest and finding the right investments. On top of that, it's also about planning for one's goals and dealing with changing circumstances along the way.

After all, while our money is in the stock market, life happens.

Or, as JPMorgan puts it, to manage the retirement income challenge, "retirees must navigate myriad risks and uncertainties," as it asks why retirement investing is so challenging.

According to the bank, more than half of all retirees left the workforce earlier than expected due to factors that were outside of their control, often related to health or organizational changes. We cannot plan for that. Or at least, to a limited extent.

Generally speaking, we're dealing with at least four major risks/factors we need to take into account when (or before) putting money to work.

  • Market risks: This is the most obvious one, as markets tend to move both up and down. The key to retirement investing is finding stocks that come with decent income, consistent dividend growth, and downside protection. As obvious as this may sound, having limited downside increases the odds that investors can sell their stocks (for whatever reason) at reasonable prices. Let's say you invest in highly cyclical stocks, you may find yourself in a 30-40% drawdown the moment you want to sell. And generally speaking, it makes sense to lower market risks the older we get, as priorities tend to shift from capital gains to income generation.
  • Longevity risk: Life is full of statistics. Technically speaking, a male who is 65 years old today has a 64% chance of making it to the age of 85. A 65-year-old female has a higher probability of making it to 90. We can use these numbers to plan, as there's a risk in underestimating one's lifespan. Some investors shift to income generation way too early and miss out on many years of potential capital gains. However, we also know that regardless of statistics, every day is a gift. In 2022, on average, more than 120 people died in car crashes in the United States, according to the CDC. Unfortunately, that's just one of many ways to skew the longevity outlook. Hence, there's also a risk of not enjoying life enough. Every dollar we invest, we cannot spend on a trip, a nice dinner, or anything else that gives us joy. Enjoying life is just as important as worrying about stocks.

  • Inflation risk: This is one of the risks we discuss almost daily, as it's the silent wealth killer. While spending tends to go down the older we get, we become more dependent on our savings and Social Security hikes to deal with inflation. Over the past ten years alone, the purchasing power of $43 has dropped by more than $10. A $1,000 in 1913 has the same purchasing power as $32 in 2025.

  • Spending variability: This is something I briefly mentioned in the bullet point above. The older we get, the more our spending patterns change. Currently, the average spending in the U.S. declines from $76 thousand per year for people aged 60-64 to $53 thousand by the time we make it past the age of 85. However, I would say that spending risks increase, as unforeseen healthcare problems could add substantial costs. The same goes for costs related to care and assisted living. There are so many things to take into account. And, to be honest, I don't even want to think about what healthcare costs could look like 40-50 years from now.

So, what's the solution?

Invest as much as possible for as long as possible in stocks with great risk/reward while enjoying life as much as possible.

The older we get, the more money we shift to income-generating investments, as we become less dependent on growing capital and more on taking care of daily expenses. However, as we just discussed, we also need to be aware that shifting to income-focused investments tends to limit growth and inflation protection.

That's one of the reasons why I always incorporate some lower-yielding dividend growers in my research, as I am 29, and because even older investors may benefit from owning more "growth."

Having said all of this, in the second part of this article, I'll shed some light on two investments that are meaningful drivers of my parents' portfolio.

It's one of the portfolios that I indirectly manage.

The only reason why I haven't discussed any major details of this portfolio is that I publish articles using my real name. While I don't mind telling the world all about my personal life, I need to be careful when I discuss other people's assets, at least for the time being.

However, I can give you a few facts that will put the investments we're about to discuss in context:

  • We're dealing with a married couple roughly 5-6 years away from legal retirement.
  • Retirement will be covered by two pensions.
  • The main goal of the dividend portfolio is to allow for slightly earlier retirement (which will cause a pension penalty) and have more buying power during retirement.
  • We try to balance the portfolio between 60-70% ETFs and 30-40% stocks, to lower the need for "maintenance."

Hence, I will be presenting two stocks.

  • One higher-yielding stock is the core of their income-generating portfolio.
  • One stock that has a decent yield and stronger growth.

Ideally, by owning and consistently adding to these two stocks, we can build a powerful portfolio that comes with both income and income growth.

Antero Midstream (AM) - Small But Powerful

It may not come as a surprise that one of my favorite income stocks is also the core holding of my parents' account.

We started buying Antero Midstream roughly two years ago, when the midstream industry didn't get the love it deserved. Nowadays, the market has warmed up to the massive potential that companies that own pipeline, storage, and gathering/processing facilities bring to the table.

Unlike actual oil and gas producers, midstream companies are not directly dependent on the prices of oil and gas. These companies generate almost all of their revenue from take-or-pay deals or similar contracts.

The biggest risk is a demand implosion and/or commodity prices that are so low that output is being restrained. The probability of that is extremely low, as it barely even happened during the pandemic.

Currently, we're dealing with more tailwinds than headwinds, as the U.S. is seeing continuing growth in oil and gas production (including natural gas liquids), a pipeline infrastructure deficit, and the strong need for high-quality income, as the rotation from growth to value stocks is coming to fruition.

With that said, Antero Midstream is somewhat of a niche player, as its operations are fully focused on Appalachia, where it owns the midstream assets of Antero Resources (AR), one of North America's largest gas producers. It is also one of the most efficient players, with breakeven prices close to $2 in natural gas. When adding more than two decades' worth of high-quality reserves (with consistent new discoveries), we get a low-risk operating environment for Antero Midstream.

It has used its strong relationship with Antero Resources and the related predictability to grow its adjusted EBITDA by 17% per year since 2013. It also boosted its return on invested capital to roughly 20%, one of the highest numbers in the entire industry, and a sign of efficient capital spending.

Especially for capital-intensive companies, having a high ROIC is important, as the worst-performing midstream companies tend to be the ones with low returns on their capital investments.

Although AM is not expanding as rapidly as its bigger peers, it is using its position in Appalachia to grow and reward shareholders.

For example, for the full year 2024, free cash flow after dividends reached a record of $250 million, allowing AM to internally finance its Marcellus bolt-on acquisition, reduce debt by almost $100 million, and still execute share buybacks.

Currently yielding 5%, Antero Midstream sees up to $730 million in free cash flow this year. This would imply a payout ratio of just 61%, one of the lowest numbers in the entire industry, as it is expected to generate between $250 million and $300 million in post-dividends free cash flow this year.

A big part of this will be used for buybacks, as the company aims to generate value through long-term dividend growth and buybacks.

As the company has a 2025E free cash flow yield of 8.4% (upper range of its guidance), we get an elevated return yield, which now has the support of a healthy balance sheet, as Antero Midstream reached its target leverage ratio earlier this year.

Unlike its bigger peers, Antero isn't dealing with major expansions. It has its assets up and running and uses smaller M&A deals to complement its network. This means it can return more money to shareholders, relatively speaking.

It also has a wildcard, which is the growing data center demand.

Because Appalachia has cheap natural gas and lots of cooling water, it is a great place to potentially build data centers.

While discussions on these opportunities are still in early stages, any future data center-related demand could provide an additional boost to AM's throughput and financial performance, without elevated CapEx needs.

Valuation-wise, Antero Midstream remains attractive, as it is expected to grow its EPS by 17% this year, potentially followed by 6% and 9% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

Using a 20x multiple, we get a fair stock price of roughly $22.40, 23% above the current price.

Because of all of these qualities, my parents are consistently adding to AM, as it's the perfect stock for long-term income.

I expect dividend growth to resume next year, as the main focus currently remains on buybacks. Once its valuation becomes less favorable, we'll likely see a switch to dividend growth.

RTX Corp. (RTX) - The Perfect Mix Of Growth And Value

RTX Corp. is one of the most important stocks across all family accounts.

We were aggressive buyers in 2023, when the stock was pressured by supply chain issues and problems with its Geared Turbofan, which resulted in grounded planes and accelerated maintenance requirements.

Initially, it sent a shockwave through the market. However, as we expected, RTX bounced back, reaching a new all-time high in 2024, fueled by a successful maintenance program and strong tailwinds in both its commercial and defense segments.

Although RTX is now at an all-time high, we still love it and continue to add on any dips, as RTX has the best of two worlds, including leading commercial operations and a huge defense platform that includes military engines, one of the most advanced missile portfolios, and countless other products.

In fact, I believe there's no better large-cap dividend stock that gives investors better access to commercial and defense customers than RTX, as it has products in almost every single airplane in the sky, including engines, avionics, landing gear, seats, and so much more.

Or, as RTX makes the case, it services everything from nose to tail on top of its defense exposure.

Currently, commercial aviation is doing what it did before the pandemic, which is growing rapidly due to secular growth from emerging markets and increasing global access to recreational and business travel.

In defense, it benefits from elevated defense spending in non-U.S. NATO nations and ongoing modernization of all allied and U.S. defense capabilities.

I also like that it's mainly operating as a supplier to major projects like the F-35 instead of being responsible as a main contractor. This allows RTX to diversify risks and apply its capabilities in a very targeted manner.

Going into this year, it had a stunning $218 billion backlog with a 1.39x book-to-bill ratio, indicating $1.39 in new orders for every dollar of completed work, which is highly indicative of elevated long-term revenue growth.

On top of the aforementioned tailwinds, the company benefits from a massive installed base. Its Collins Aerospace segment, for example, has an installed base of $160 billion, with more than half of these planes being out of warranty. That's a blessing for aftermarket sales and demand for new equipment.

Its Raytheon segment, which is entirely focused on defense, has $63 billion in backlog and a 1.48x book-to-bill ratio, with strong demand for its missile and radar systems.

Key growth areas include integrated air and missile defense ("IAMD"), with products like Patriot, NASAMS, Coyote counter-UAS, and Tomahawk missiles all enjoying elevated interest due to global conflicts.

In order to turn this demand into value, the company has three strategic priorities.

  1. First, meeting customer demand remains the company's main priority, especially as production ramps up across commercial aerospace and defense. To achieve this, the company has invested heavily in supply chain resilience, qualifying multiple suppliers for critical components to mitigate disruptions. As a student of supply chains, I can say this is one of the most straightforward and effective ways to mitigate risks.
  2. Second, as one can imagine, innovation is key to the company's long-term growth. Hence, the company is investing $2.5 billion in capital expenditures in 2025, with a focus on next-generation technologies like the (commercial) GTF Advantage engine and advanced defense systems.
  3. Third, RTX continues to refine operations through its CORE operating system, which is a continuous improvement framework that has already driven significant efficiencies. To give you an example, last year, the company achieved 11% organic sales growth with only a 2% increase in headcount.

When adding that most NATO nations are now boosting defense spending beyond 2% of their GDPs, we get a fantastic environment for RTX to thrive.

That's great news for its investors as well.

With regard to its dividend, RTX yields 1.9%.

This dividend has a five-year CAGR of 6.4% and a payout ratio of just 41%.

On May 3, the company hiked by 6.8%. I expect the next few years to see higher dividend growth, as elevated demand and company measures have translated to a fantastic earnings outlook. That's also why I still like its valuation.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, the company is expected to grow its EPS by 7% this year, potentially followed by 11-12% growth in 2026 and 2027. Beyond these years, I expect growth to remain in double-digit territory, supported by high demand, improving margins, and buybacks.

Applying a 22x multiple to one of its strongest growth streaks ever (including pre-merger United Technologies), we get a fair stock price of nearly $170, 28% above the current price.

While RTX currently yields 1.9%, if we assume 8% annual dividend growth, we could see a 4.1% yield on cost in ten years. Moreover, if capital gains are similar (I believe they will be higher), they can still sell RTX if needed and move more money into higher-yielding investments.

That's why I/we like dividend growers with both income and dividend growth potential so much.

Hence, we're buying more on weakness, especially on larger dips/corrections.

Takeaway

Investing isn't just about picking stocks. It's about balancing risks, adapting to life's unexpected events, and enjoying the journey as much as possible, despite the fact that market swings, inflation, our personal longevity, and shifting spending needs all demand our attention.

Hence, my approach is simple, which is to stay invested in quality stocks for as long as possible, leaning into growth early and shifting toward income later.

For my parents' portfolio, that means a mix of steady income like Antero Midstream, with its 5% yield and buyback potential, and RTX, which offers a perfect mix of growth and income in the aerospace industry.

I believe both offer compelling upside while fitting their near-retirement goals.

Risks To My Thesis

Antero Midstream faces gas demand and regulatory risks, while RTX could see pressure from defense cuts, new supply chain issues, or weakness in commercial aviation demand.

On top of that, inflation, recessions, or general market weakness risks apply.

data.title: Lightspeed Commerce: Supplier Integration Might Not Be The Best POS Strategy
data.description:
In the quantitative department and according to my analysis, Lightspeed looks like a good pick. But if we look closely at LSPD strategy we can infer that it is not working as expected. In addition, competition in the POS business is high because it encompasses Fintechs and Banks. Because the quantitative and the qualitative fields don’t define Lightspeed as a buy, I’m rating it as a hold.

Background

Lightspeed (LSPD) is a company focused on deploying POS systems and payment processing in its merchants. Initially Lightspeed focused on developing the Point-of-Sale software, improving the management of inventory, sales, orders and analytics, in retail, which is its biggest revenue segment, but also in hospitality. Then Lightspeed expanded to payment processing and more recently is enforcing merchants using the POS software to use its payment processing system. Merchants that don’t migrate to LSPD’s payments are charged more fees, while the ones that join LSPD’s payments get a negotiated take rate that looks to match or beat the previous third-party take rate.

Lightspeed has a strong presence in specific retail businesses, such as golf courses, developing vertically integrated solutions that enhance its POS system and protect its moat around those specific retailers.

To improve vertical integration in its retailers, in 2021 LSPD acquired NuOrder for 425 million. Considering that LSPD market cap sits at 1.3 billion dollars, it was a massive acquisition and strategic move. NuOrder works with more than 500 thousand retailers and 3000 brands, allowing retailers to order and sell, in a B2B format, goods from the international brands.

Strategy

Improve retailers’ vertical integration, by acquiring NuOrder and integrating other suppliers. By integrating and buying supply-driven platforms, Lightspeed is improving the ordering capabilities, inventory management and automating analytics processes. If the retailer uses NuOrder to supply its business it will be able to understand how much inventory is available in NuOrder, making the retailer buy more inventory if the good is being oversold in store and there is a lot of competition for it in NuOrder. The POS system and the supplier are directly connected, so the retailer can measure quickly inventory in store and in transit, leading to the automation of back-office processes and better visibility of the overall inventory. In addition, the orders are integrated in the POS system. As a result, retailers have more visibility of when the order is arriving at the store.

In the latest earnings call the company’s management said this:

Second, as you know, we've connected effectively our new order wholesale platform with our point of sale. And so at the point of sale level now, you can actually see the inventory of your suppliers, which is really a unique proposition that no one is able to offer and we're really pleased with the progress there. We've seen over 2,000 POS customers connecting to the supplier network and that number is growing significantly.

NuOrder acquisition brought a large number of retailers to Lightspeed’s ecosystem, facilitating cross-selling opportunities for LSPD’s POS and payment processing, and for NuOrder supply. Despite the heavy price tag, NuOrder acquisition is bringing differentiation to the POS software capabilities and opening a large market for LSPD.

Financials

I would like to compare Lightspeed to Shift4 (FOUR) because both follow different strategies and work in similar segments of the market. Shift4 executes more horizontal acquisitions, expanding internationally by buying new companies, but also does, with less intensity, vertical acquisitions, for example, recently acquiring Givex that specializes in loyalty and gift cards solutions. Lightspeed engages more in vertical integration, acquiring NuOrder for a large sum, but also enters in horizontal acquisitions, such as Vend, an Asia-Pacific retailer POS provider.

Lightspeed has much higher gross margins because it generates 1/3 of the revenue via POS SaaS subscriptions, while Shift4 generates 1/10 of the revenue with SaaS subscriptions. SaaS subscriptions revenue has higher gross margins than payment processing revenue. On the other hand, Shift4 has increased very substantially its gross margins YoY while Lightspeed decreased them slightly.

Shift4 has a much higher Gross Profit/Employed Capital, however, Lightspeed has been increasing this value significantly in the last year. Despite Shift4 being known for a large number of acquisitions and acquisition fueled growth, it has much less Goodwill as a percentage of total assets than Lightspeed.

If we look at the growth estimates, we can see that Shift4 is projected to grow revenue at a faster pace than Lightspeed. Yet, the latter has been restructuring to balance revenue growth with opex. As a result, it will increase the non-GAAP earnings growth.

Shift4 is much more valued than Lightspeed using a Price/Gross Profit multiple. The revenue is growing faster, its gross profit generation is more efficient than Lightspeed, while boasting very high EBITDA margins.

Opportunities

LSPD keeps innovating and expanding its POS capabilities. One area where I believe they can make a difference while increasing market acceptance is by deploying advanced analytics in the merchant’s retailers. The company recently introduced the Retail Insights tool. It enables the retailer to forecast sales, manage inventory and orders by using ML and heuristics. The management of the store is much more automated and streamlined, being a perfect solution for mid-sized retailers that work with multiple stores .

In the latest earnings call, the company’s management said this:

On the product and technology front, I'll highlight how recently introduced software offerings are already contributing to our software revenue growth, as well as some of the newer releases this quarter. The retail insights module, which we launched last quarter, is already contributing to our software growth and is enabling merchants to improve their gross margins by a 16% reduction in out-of-stock days for popular items.

Lightspeed also developed a tool that allows restaurants to benchmark to the overall industry (serviced by Lightspeed), providing a competing solution to the more established Toast (TOST). I would like to see LSPD develop a similar solution for each of its retail segments, a high-value proposition for the store owners.

Risks

LSPD tends to invest large sums in premium acquisitions that might not translate into good capital allocation.

The company is not profitable yet while its growth is lower than Shift4’s, if growth contracts, we could see a major downgrade in the company's multiples.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. To be conservative, I assumed a starting FCF margin of 1%, that expands to 8% (slightly lower than Shift4’s current TTM 12% FCF margin). To generate the terminal multiple, I’m using a terminal growth rate of 4.7%. The terminal growth rate is obtained summing a real GDP growth rate of 2.5% and an inflation rate (since 2000) of 2.2%, resulting in the number 4.7%. I also take into consideration the share count to calculate the FCF per share. It expands by 2.5% every year. With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current share price.

According to the model inputs, the market is pricing a growth that starts at 9.8% in 2025 and reduces to 5% every year, reaching 6.1% in 2034. The company looks cheap for the growth that analysts are predicting.

Conclusion

LSPD is executing its differentiation strategy by integrating vertically with its merchants’ suppliers. For now, that strategy as translated into low performance, with only 2000 merchants being integrated with their suppliers' data, from a pool of more than 150 000 merchants. Lightspeed's costly acquisition of NuOrder may not have delivered the impact the company had anticipated. The company looks cheap from a DCF analysis perspective. Despite this, bad capital allocation poses risks for future share returns.

I like that Lightspeed is trying to differentiate by being the most vertically integrated POS supplier, but maybe, it is not the best strategy for a POS provider. Since my strategy is to be selective while buying and holding for the long term, LSPD doesn’t fit my style due to questionable strategy and capital allocation. I rate LSPD a hold.

data.title: Intel: Don't Bet On A Quick Turnaround
data.description:
Intel’s (NASDAQ:INTC) new CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, recently gave a keynote presentation where he laid out his vision for the company. This included a wide range of topics. Some of the most positive aspects of the speech were a more innovative culture and having a startup mindset. Earlier, Tan had mentioned that he would need to address the slow-moving and bloated middle management. Deep job cuts could help change the culture and allow the CEO to invest in areas that require greater priority. However, Tan also reiterated the approach of his predecessor in building a strong foundry business. I believe the foundry business would require a very long time to deliver results that Intel does not have. The focus of the foundry business will also take away crucial resources that are needed to compete in AI chips. In the previous article, I upgraded Intel stock to Hold. Since then, the stock has seen a 40% jump and has settled back to the low $20s range.

Intel has entered the risk production for its latest 18A chips and is planning on commercial production in the second half of this year. There have been 27 downward EPS revisions in the last 90 days, compared to only one upgrade. This shows that Wall Street is still not convinced by the approach of Intel and will take a few more quarters to see any positive improvement. The EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $1.14, which gives the stock a forward PE of 19.3. This is quite high when we look at other semiconductor stocks that have a better growth trajectory.

Pros and cons of the recent keynote event

The new CEO laid out his vision for Intel in a recent keynote event. There were many positive factors mentioned during this speech. One of the best was a desire to change the culture of Intel. Instead of focusing on some products, the new CEO spent a lot of time discussing the new culture that is required at Intel. The word “culture” was mentioned more than 20 times in the speech, which shows the strong inclination of the new CEO to change things from the bottom up. He also mentioned having a new startup mindset in the company, which can drive faster innovation. Previously, Tan has already mentioned that Intel has a very bloated middle management. Hence, it is very likely that we could see deep job cuts in Intel over the next few quarters. This would allow the management to invest resources in more important areas.

Figure: Recent EPS revisions of Intel. Source: Seeking Alpha

Over the last 90 days, Intel has seen 27 downward revisions and only 1 upward revision. This clearly shows the poor sentiment towards the stock on Wall Street. It will take a number of quarters to change this trajectory, and we should not expect any quick results from the new CEO.

A big negative within the keynote speech was a continuation of the foundry plan laid out by the previous CEO. I believe it would be very difficult for Intel to compete with TSMC (TSM) and build a strong market share in this segment. The foundry business requires massive investments and a long time to deliver results. Intel does not have this much time, and poor sentiment on Wall Street could also limit the ability of the company to absorb any headwinds in this segment. Intel’s CEO mentioned that he is “equally committed to building a strong foundry.”

Intel has already delayed the Ohio chipmaking site to the next decade. Initially, this fab was supposed to start in 2025. This later got pushed to 2027, and now it has been delayed to 2030. It is not clear how the new CEO intends to deal with these delays. Another big disadvantage of focusing on the foundry business is that it takes limited resources away from the AI segment. Intel has fallen behind Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD) in building an attractive AI chip business. Intel would need to make a lot of investment to build a strong AI chip business. Hence, any diversion of resources in foundry business can be a big negative for the overall potential of the company.

Big job cuts and R&D expense

Intel’s new CEO has already mentioned that the company has a bloated middle management that moves very slowly. This hurts the ability of the company to deliver new products aggressively. Intel has already announced massive job cuts of 15% of its workforce in August 2024. The focus of the new CEO on changing the culture of Intel and removing excess staff could mean we see another big job cut announcement from Intel. I believe we could hear a job cut announcement of at least 20% to 30% in the near term. This will improve the cash flow for the company and also lift the EPS over the next few quarters.

Figure: Intel’s EPS estimate in the next few fiscal years. Source: Seeking Alpha

For the fiscal year ending Dec 2026, Intel is expected to deliver EPS of $1.14 which gives the stock a forward PE of 19.30. This PE is still quite high when we compare it with other chipmakers. Another negative is the massive gap between the lowest and highest forward EPS estimates for Intel. The lowest EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $0.60, while the highest is $2.00. Hence, the highest forward EPS estimate is more than thrice the lowest EPS estimate. This shows the massive uncertainty in the ability of Intel to deliver good EPS in the next few quarters.

Intel’s R&D expense is significantly higher than that of Nvidia, AMD and TSM. In the trailing twelve months, Intel’s R&D expense was $16.5 billion, while Nvidia was $12.9 billion, AMD was $6.5 billion, and TSM was $6.4 billion. This shows that despite massive research expenses, Intel has not been able to deliver attractive products. Massive job cuts at Intel could change this issue by bringing in new talent that delivers better results.

Maintain a “Hold” rating

In the previous article, I upgraded Intel’s stock to a Hold rating from a Sell rating. The main reason behind this upgrade was a change in future plans by the new management. Since the upgrade, Intel stock went up by 40% to $27 and has again settled to its previous range of $20 to $22. Despite a massive culture change at Intel, it will take some time to see actual results. As mentioned above, we will also need to see how the new management deals with the foundry business. A big investment in foundry could further delay the turnaround for the company. Job cuts are a positive factor, but we need to see improvement in the product lineup to build strong bullish momentum. It is unlikely that Wall Street is going to change its sentiment towards Intel only on the announcements of Intel.

Even for two fiscal years ahead, Intel’s forward PE multiple of 13.2 is not significantly cheaper than other chip stocks. AMD is trading at $103 with an EPS estimate of $7.5 for the two fiscal years ahead. This gives the stock a forward PE of only 13.7, despite having a better AI product line than Intel. Even TSM is trading at a forward PE of less than 15. All these companies have better growth potential, while Intel is trying to make a strong turnaround. We could see sideways momentum in Intel stock for a few more quarters before any concrete results are reported by the company.

Investor Takeaway

In the recent keynote event, Intel’s new CEO Lip-Bu Tan has laid out an aggressive vision for the company. He mentioned changing the culture of the company and will likely make deep job cuts to reduce the massive middle management. However, he also mentioned that Intel will continue to focus on the foundry business, which can take a long time to deliver results. Intel has already delayed the launch of its Ohio plant till 2030.

The R&D expense of Intel is still larger than Nvidia, AMD, and TSM. If the new management makes big changes in talent acquisition, Intel has the resources to build a strong product lineup. This will take a few more quarters, which is why a Hold rating is given to the stock. Intel stock does not have a big discount in PE when compared to other competitors. This further reduces the upside for the stock from the current price point.

data.title: Friday: Tariffs, U.S. Jobs, Powell, And Melting Equities
data.description:
Overview

There appear to be two-forces at work that have helped the US dollar recover. First, as the market continues to debate whether the reciprocal tariffs are a negotiating ploy and in this tug-of-war of sorts, President Trump's declaration that he is open to "phenomenal offers" plays into that view. Still, the fact that Israel got rid of all of its tariffs on the US and still was hit with a 17% levy is notable. Second, there may be some position-adjustment ahead of the US jobs report. So far, the weakness in the US labor market appears in the soft, survey data, not the hard data, like weekly initial jobless claims. The rise in continuing claims is consistent with slowing in employment. A few hours after the jobs report, Fed Chair Powell will speak about the economy. This could be another source of volatility. However, we suspect he will be reluctant to signal much concern about the volatility of the stock market, i.e., no resurrection of the simplistic--stock market falls sharply, and Fed will cut" so-called Fed put.

The US dollar is firmer against all the G10 currencies but the Swiss franc. German factory orders disappointed, and BOJ Governor Ueda recognized the unsettled global environments. Emerging market currencies are mixed, with Asia-Pacific outperforming Central Europe. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at its highest for the year. Few equity markets have escaped the selling pressure and US index futures are off 0.5%-1.0%, which warns of the gap lower opening. Bonds are still the safe haven. European 10-year yields are off 6-10 bps, and the peripheral premiums are widening. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is off eight basis points to about 3.95%. Gold, ironically, is trading more like a risk asset and is off 0.75% today, but above yesterday's low (~$3054). May WTI has extended yesterday's dramatic sell-off, spurred by OPEC+ decision to boost sales around three-times more than expected and broader growth concerns. The contract reached about $64.25, its lowest since last September.

USD: The dollar fell sharply and broadly yesterday. The Dollar Index dropped by almost 1.7%, its largest drop since November 2022. It fell from a high Wednesday near 104.30 to a low a near 101.25 but managed to settle above slightly102.00. The sell-off was so sharp that the Dollar Index spent most of the European and North American session below three standard deviations from the 20-day moving average (~112.20). The Bollinger Bands are set two standard deviations away from the 20-day moving average. Three is an extreme event, if the returns are not normally distributed. After sliding back to nearly101.50, DXY recovered to the first area of technical resistance may be in the 102.40-50 area, near where the lower Bollinger Band is found. Above there, the 102.80-103.00 may offer stronger resistance.
